EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mid-Atlantic was in between a cold front approaching form the Appalachian Mountains and Hurricane Arthur off the Delaware Coast. Warm and humid conditions were across the region and showers and thunderstorms led to heavy rain. Unstable conditions led to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree reported down blocking North River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was over the western Atlantic Ocean and southerly flow brought hot and humid conditions into the Mid-Atlantic leading to a unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms moved across the region in the afternoon and evening and some thunderstorms were capable of damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were power lines down at Polo Green and Rock Cliff Drives.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough tapped into Gulf and Atlantic moisture which led to heavy rain across the Mid Atlantic. Tropical-like conditions resulted in showers and thunderstorms to persist before a cold front moved through later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river at Bunker Hill reached flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream was out of its banks and at least six inches of water was reported to be flowing across Highway 220 just north of the Grant and Pendleton County lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Back Creek was out of its banks and surrounding a residence on Puffenburger Lane in Hedgesville. A water rescue was ongoing and a rescue boat was on the scene.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped below 32 degrees at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

An upper level disturbance moved across the Mid Atlantic in the morning. A jet axis coincided with the disturbance and snow showers moved across the region. The most accumulation occurred east of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 3 inches or more were measured at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving east across the upper Ohio Valley. Severe thunderstorms occurred in parts of western Pennsylvania, as well as Garrett county Maryland, and Preston county in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported numerous trees down.

Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.
A fast moving cold front moved across the Mid Atlantic. Snowfall accumulation was confined to areas near the Mason Dixon line were temperatures were well below 32 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 2 inches or more was measured at surrounding locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 2 inches or more was measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wedge of cold air was in place across the Potomac Highlands and Northern Maryland while warm air moved into the region from the Tennessee Valley. This led to overrunning and showers across the area. Freezing rain occurred for areas with surface temperatures below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of a trace or more was measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rock slide damaged a long narrow cinder block building made up of mostly garages.  Many years ago, a 20 to 25 foot high vertical cliff was made when a hillside was cut away.  The building was built almost right in front of that cliff.  Over the years, the freezing and thawing caused the cliff to become unstable.  The building has been flooded numerous times from runoff off the cliff.  ||A large section of the vertical cliff finally gave way during the morning of the 19th.  Several large six by six feet chunks of rock slid down the steep embankment.  The slide damaged some motorcycles and cars inside. Nobody was hurt.

As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in the southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into West Virginia during the predawn.||The steadiest snow during the daylight hours occurred across central counties of West Virginia.  The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario.  In some counties, the precipitation started as a brief period of sleet and rain before changing to wet snow.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the lowland counties, with a lowland maximum around 7 to 8 inches in parts of Roane County on northeast into Braxton and Lewis Counties.  In the central mountain counties, 5 to 9 inches of snow accumulation was common.   A few specific sites from cooperative observers included 8 inches at French Creek.  Spencer, Weston, and Bartow all observed with 7 inches. An unofficial report of around 10 to 12 inches was received from the Whitmer vicinity in eastern Randolph Coonty.  ||Further south, much less snow fell, with only an inch or less observed around Fayetteville, Oak Hill, and Beckley.||Even in the heavier snow areas, most roads remained just wet for the first several hours of the storm, then became slushy during the night.

After a major January thaw with temperatures reaching into the lower 60s on the 1st, a cold front gradually sank through western portions of West Virginia during the afternoon on the 2nd.  Spotty light rain fell with the front. ||Meanwhile, upper level support arrived from the southwest to enhance the precipitation overnight into the 3rd.  The light rain changed to freezing rain and sleet, then to snow.  The snow decreased during the mid morning to midday time frame on the 3rd.||Heavy snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common across the mid Ohio Valley into north central counties. A few public reports of 9 to 10 inches were received from Tyler County. Further south and east in West Virginia, snow amounts were much less.

Snow spread north during the afternoon of the 12th, reaching into extreme southern West Virginia by 1600E.  The snow was associated with a developing coastal storm that was still down over northern Florida.  Meanwhile, a cold high pressure ridging down the eastern seaboard kept plenty of cold air in the lower atmosphere..||The snow slowly moved north during the evening hours, but struggled to move northwest. The snow began in the Charleston and Elkins vicinity around 1930E.  Temperatures were mostly in the 20s at the onset of the snow. ||This pattern will eventually leave a strong east to west snow gradient in the western lowlands of West Virginia. ||The heaviest snow during the evening hours remained over the southern coal fields and worked northeast across the southern mountains and into the Greenbrier Valley.||The coastal storm was centered along the Virginia and North Carolina coast line by dawn on the 13th. The wrap around snow on the western side of the strong mid and upper level cyclonic circulation caused the snow to continue to fall, and in many counties increase after dawn into midday on the 13th.  The snow finally ended during the mid and late afternoon.||Total storm snow accumulations of 10 to 18 inches were common over the southern coal fields of McDowell and Wyoming Counties on northeast to include Raleigh, Fayette. Nicholas, Webster, Upshur, Pocahontas, Randolph, and even into southern Barbour County.  A snow accumulation maximum on either side of 20 inches was seen in parts of Randolph, Pocahontas, Webster, Nicholas Counties, Upshur, and even extreme southern Barbour County.  For example, in the Queens to Hemlock vicinity, near the Upshur and Randolph County border, a spotter measured 22 inches.  The postmaster at Helvetia measured around 19 inches.   ||A total storm accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were more common from Mingo County on northeast through Logan, Boone, Kanawha, Clay, Roane, Calhoun, Braxton, Gilmer, Lewis, and northern Barbour County. Harrison County had an accumulation mostly in the 6 to 8 inch range.  On the western fringe of the heavier snow, Lincoln and Putnam Counties had 4 to 7 inches of snow accumulation. Taylor County had less snow with only 3 to 5 inches of snow.  Even less accumulation fell in the Ohio River Valley Counties.  ||From official cooperative observers the snow accumulation was 15 inches at Hawks Nest and Elkins, 13 inches at Buckeye, Bartow, Frost, Summersville, and Hacker Valley, while 12 inches accumulated at Snowshoe.  ||Some unofficial snow accumulations included Oak Hill and Runa with 14 inches, Green Bank and Craigsville with 17 inches, and Montrose with 20 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of five inches or more were measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario.  In some counties, the precipitation started as a brief period of sleet and rain before changing to wet snow.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the lowland counties, with a lowland maximum around 7 to 8 inches in parts of Roane County on northeast into Braxton and Lewis Counties.  In the central mountain counties, 5 to 9 inches of snow accumulation was common.   A few specific sites from cooperative observers included 8 inches at French Creek.  Spencer, Weston, and Bartow all observed with 7 inches. An unofficial report of around 10 to 12 inches was received from the Whitmer vicinity in eastern Randolph Coonty.  ||Further south, much less snow fell, with only an inch or less observed around Fayetteville, Oak Hill, and Beckley.||Even in the heavier snow areas, most roads remained just wet for the first several hours of the storm, then became slushy during the night.

As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in the southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into West Virginia during the predawn.||The steadiest snow during the daylight hours occurred across central counties of West Virginia.  The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

Meanwhile, a cold high pressure ridging down the eastern seaboard kept plenty of cold air in the lower atmosphere..||The snow slowly moved north during the evening hours, but struggled to move northwest. The snow began in the Charleston and Elkins vicinity around 1930E.  Temperatures were mostly in the 20s at the onset of the snow. ||This pattern will eventually leave a strong east to west snow gradient in the western lowlands of West Virginia. ||The heaviest snow during the evening hours remained over the southern coal fields and worked northeast across the southern mountains and into the Greenbrier Valley.||The coastal storm was centered along the Virginia and North Carolina coast line by dawn on the 13th. The wrap around snow on the western side of the strong mid and upper level cyclonic circulation caused the snow to continue to fall, and in many counties increase after dawn into midday on the 13th.  The snow finally ended during the mid and late afternoon.||Total storm snow accumulations of 10 to 18 inches were common over the southern coal fields of McDowell and Wyoming Counties on northeast to include Raleigh, Fayette. Nicholas, Webster, Upshur, Pocahontas, Randolph, and even into southern Barbour County.  A snow accumulation maximum on either side of 20 inches was seen in parts of Randolph, Pocahontas, Webster, Nicholas Counties, Upshur, and even extreme southern Barbour County.  For example, in the Queens to Hemlock vicinity, near the Upshur and Randolph County border, a spotter measured 22 inches.  The postmaster at Helvetia measured around 19 inches.   ||A total storm accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were more common from Mingo County on northeast through Logan, Boone, Kanawha, Clay, Roane, Calhoun, Braxton, Gilmer, Lewis, and northern Barbour County. Harrison County had an accumulation mostly in the 6 to 8 inch range.  On the western fringe of the heavier snow, Lincoln and Putnam Counties had 4 to 7 inches of snow accumulation. Taylor County had less snow with only 3 to 5 inches of snow.  Even less accumulation fell in the Ohio River Valley Counties.  ||From official cooperative observers the snow accumulation was 15 inches at Hawks Nest and Elkins, 13 inches at Buckeye, Bartow, Frost, Summersville, and Hacker Valley, while 12 inches accumulated at Snowshoe.  ||Some unofficial snow accumulations included Oak Hill and Runa with 14 inches, Green Bank and Craigsville with 17 inches, and Montrose with 20 inches.

As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in the southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into West Virginia during the predawn.||The steadiest snow during the daylight hours occurred across central counties of West Virginia.  The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario.  In some counties, the precipitation started as a brief period of sleet and rain before changing to wet snow.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the lowland counties, with a lowland maximum around 7 to 8 inches in parts of Roane County on northeast into Braxton and Lewis Counties.  In the central mountain counties, 5 to 9 inches of snow accumulation was common.   A few specific sites from cooperative observers included 8 inches at French Creek.  Spencer, Weston, and Bartow all observed with 7 inches. An unofficial report of around 10 to 12 inches was received from the Whitmer vicinity in eastern Randolph Coonty.  ||Further south, much less snow fell, with only an inch or less observed around Fayetteville, Oak Hill, and Beckley.||Even in the heavier snow areas, most roads remained just wet for the first several hours of the storm, then became slushy during the night.

Colder air was moving in aloft on the 25th, as a mid level disturbance passed.  Frequent snow showers and a few snow squalls formed over West Virginia on the 25th.  Daytime temperatures remained in the 20s across high elevations with 30s in the river valleys.  The last of the heavier snow showers occurred after sunset on the 25th, and were more efficient in accumulating.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common in the central and southern mountain counties, with an inch common across western lowland counties. Snowshoe measured a 6 inch accumulation.  Temperatures were in the teens for dawn on the 26th.||County school districts in the southern and central mountain counties canceled or delayed classes for the 26th.

Even less accumulation fell in the Ohio River Valley Counties.  ||From official cooperative observers the snow accumulation was 15 inches at Hawks Nest and Elkins, 13 inches at Buckeye, Bartow, Frost, Summersville, and Hacker Valley, while 12 inches accumulated at Snowshoe.  ||Some unofficial snow accumulations included Oak Hill and Runa with 14 inches, Green Bank and Craigsville with 17 inches, and Montrose with 20 inches.

A strengthening low pressure system moved north into eastern Iowa and Wisconsin during the evening hours on the 20th.  The attending cold front raced east, up the Ohio River Valley.  Low level winds at 3 to 5 thousand feet were blowing at 50 to 70 mph.  Ahead of the front, temperatures were in the 60s. Surface dew points reached into the lower 50s just ahead of the convection.||A narrow, but well defined, squall line formed on the leading edge of the prefrontal convection. It was oriented north to south while moving into western West Virginia after 0000E on the 21st.  Strong wind gusts were associated with the squall line, mostly in the 40 to 50 mph.  A few favored locations had stronger gusts.||Over 1300 customers lost electricity in Wayne County, around 1200 in Mingo County, and around 1000 in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 9 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 30 degrees below zero was recorded near Weirton the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As colder air was filtering south, a strong mid level disturbance moved up the Ohio River.  Only a weak low pressure system was reflected at the surface, which passed to the south. Snow developed in the southwestern Ohio by 0000E on the 21st.  The snow moved east, into West Virginia during the predawn.||The steadiest snow during the daylight hours occurred across central counties of West Virginia.  The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a major January thaw with temperatures reaching into the lower 60s on the 1st, a cold front gradually sank through western portions of West Virginia during the afternoon on the 2nd.  Spotty light rain fell with the front. ||Meanwhile, upper level support arrived from the southwest to enhance the precipitation overnight into the 3rd.  The light rain changed to freezing rain and sleet, then to snow.  The snow decreased during the mid morning to midday time frame on the 3rd.||Heavy snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common across the mid Ohio Valley into north central counties. A few public reports of 9 to 10 inches were received from Tyler County. Further south and east in West Virginia, snow amounts were much less.

The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario.  In some counties, the precipitation started as a brief period of sleet and rain before changing to wet snow.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the lowland counties, with a lowland maximum around 7 to 8 inches in parts of Roane County on northeast into Braxton and Lewis Counties.  In the central mountain counties, 5 to 9 inches of snow accumulation was common.   A few specific sites from cooperative observers included 8 inches at French Creek.  Spencer, Weston, and Bartow all observed with 7 inches. An unofficial report of around 10 to 12 inches was received from the Whitmer vicinity in eastern Randolph Coonty.  ||Further south, much less snow fell, with only an inch or less observed around Fayetteville, Oak Hill, and Beckley.||Even in the heavier snow areas, most roads remained just wet for the first several hours of the storm, then became slushy during the night.

A band of snow fell on either side of the early morning commute to work and school across portions of western and central West Virginia.  Accumulations were only 1 to 3 inches.  Numerous accidents were reported, including on portions of Interstate 77 between Parkersburg and Ripley, and along Interstate 79 in Harrison County.  A few counties closed school for the day. ||Even in the aftermath, a school bus in northern Harrison County slid off Joetown Road near Wyatt.  A coal truck may have been involved in the accident.  The bus was returning children home from school that afternoon.  The bus went down an embankment with its front end coming to rest in Bingamon Creek.  All 27 students and the driver were not injured and escaped from the rear door.

A strengthening low pressure system moved north into eastern Iowa and Wisconsin during the evening hours on the 20th.  The attending cold front raced east, up the Ohio River Valley.  Low level winds at 3 to 5 thousand feet were blowing at 50 to 70 mph.  Ahead of the front, temperatures were in the 60s. Surface dew points reached into the lower 50s just ahead of the convection.||A narrow, but well defined, squall line formed on the leading edge of the prefrontal convection. It was oriented north to south while moving into western West Virginia after 0000E on the 21st.  Strong wind gusts were associated with the squall line, mostly in the 40 to 50 mph.  A few favored locations had stronger gusts.||Over 1300 customers lost electricity in Wayne County, around 1200 in Mingo County, and around 1000 in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

Colder air was moving in aloft on the 25th, as a mid level disturbance passed.  Frequent snow showers and a few snow squalls formed over West Virginia on the 25th.  Daytime temperatures remained in the 20s across high elevations with 30s in the river valleys.  The last of the heavier snow showers occurred after sunset on the 25th, and were more efficient in accumulating.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common in the central and southern mountain counties, with an inch common across western lowland counties. Snowshoe measured a 6 inch accumulation.  Temperatures were in the teens for dawn on the 26th.||County school districts in the southern and central mountain counties canceled or delayed classes for the 26th.

Low pressure developed along an arctic boundary moving into northern West Virginia and strengthened as it moved to the Mid Atlantic coast. This produced heavy snow across the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania, Marion, Monongalia, Tucker and Preston counties in West Virginia, and Garret county Maryland. Snowfall totals were from 8 to 12 inches, with the highest amounts near a foot across the higher elevations of Preston, Garret and Tucker counties. Amounts near 8 inches were reported in Morgantown northeast into the Chestnut and Laurel Ridges of Pennsylvania. Lesser amounts from 3 to 6 inches fell across westward toward Wetzel county in West Virginia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of five inches or more were measured at surrounding locations.

The snow moved east, into West Virginia during the predawn.||The steadiest snow during the daylight hours occurred across central counties of West Virginia.  The snow diminished to fluffy snow showers during the evening, as temperatures dropped into the teens.  North winds also blew the snow around during the late afternoon and early evening.  In the wake of the storm, drier arctic air took control.  ||Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were widespread.  Accumulations of 8 to 10 inches occurred in portions of Braxton County on east into the central mountain counties.  A few of the heaviest snow accumulations from cooperative observers included 9 inches at Hacker Valley and Buckhannon, with 8 inches measured at Sutton, Gassaway, Mt Nebo, and Snowshoe.||A 79 year old Nicholas County man, who suffered with dementia, is believed to have frozen to death. He was last seen on the 20th, the day before the snow arrived.  He apparently made a wrong turn, drove several miles along a remote mountain road near Muddlety. His vehicle got stuck in an old snow cover. He apparently spun the wheels so much that his car caught fire. He walked off and his body was not found until the 23rd. Temperatures were in the 20s during the daylight hours of the 21st, then dropped to below zero that night. The date of his death was estimated on the 21st.

Late on the 16th, a low pressure storm system passed east across the Tennessee Valley, while strong high pressure was located over the Great Lakes and southern Ontario.  In some counties, the precipitation started as a brief period of sleet and rain before changing to wet snow.  The snow diminished during the predawn hours on the 17th.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the lowland counties, with a lowland maximum around 7 to 8 inches in parts of Roane County on northeast into Braxton and Lewis Counties.  In the central mountain counties, 5 to 9 inches of snow accumulation was common.   A few specific sites from cooperative observers included 8 inches at French Creek.  Spencer, Weston, and Bartow all observed with 7 inches. An unofficial report of around 10 to 12 inches was received from the Whitmer vicinity in eastern Randolph Coonty.  ||Further south, much less snow fell, with only an inch or less observed around Fayetteville, Oak Hill, and Beckley.||Even in the heavier snow areas, most roads remained just wet for the first several hours of the storm, then became slushy during the night.

Low pressure developed along an arctic boundary moving into northern West Virginia and strengthened as it moved to the Mid Atlantic coast. This produced heavy snow across the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania, Marion, Monongalia, Tucker and Preston counties in West Virginia, and Garret county Maryland. Snowfall totals were from 8 to 12 inches, with the highest amounts near a foot across the higher elevations of Preston, Garret and Tucker counties. Amounts near 8 inches were reported in Morgantown northeast into the Chestnut and Laurel Ridges of Pennsylvania. Lesser amounts from 3 to 6 inches fell across westward toward Wetzel county in West Virginia.

An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 25th. A heavy band of snow with the front reduced visibilities to below one quarter mile for a time as it crossed the region. Over a 12 to 18 hour period, snow showers ahead of the front, combined with several lake enhanced and up-slope snow bands behind it, produced a general 3 to 5 inches of snow across the region, with 4 to 8 inches of snow in the higher ridges of West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the county.

Colder air was moving in aloft on the 25th, as a mid level disturbance passed.  Frequent snow showers and a few snow squalls formed over West Virginia on the 25th.  Daytime temperatures remained in the 20s across high elevations with 30s in the river valleys.  The last of the heavier snow showers occurred after sunset on the 25th, and were more efficient in accumulating.  Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were common in the central and southern mountain counties, with an inch common across western lowland counties. Snowshoe measured a 6 inch accumulation.  Temperatures were in the teens for dawn on the 26th.||County school districts in the southern and central mountain counties canceled or delayed classes for the 26th.

After a major January thaw with temperatures reaching into the lower 60s on the 1st, a cold front gradually sank through western portions of West Virginia during the afternoon on the 2nd.  Spotty light rain fell with the front. ||Meanwhile, upper level support arrived from the southwest to enhance the precipitation overnight into the 3rd.  The light rain changed to freezing rain and sleet, then to snow.  The snow decreased during the mid morning to midday time frame on the 3rd.||Heavy snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common across the mid Ohio Valley into north central counties. A few public reports of 9 to 10 inches were received from Tyler County. Further south and east in West Virginia, snow amounts were much less.

Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.
High pressure over the region resulted in light and variable winds. Moisture advection from a coastal low pressure combining with the light winds resulted in an extended period of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations in surrounding counties reported one-quarter mile visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region resulted in light and variable winds. Moisture advection from a coastal low pressure combining with the light winds resulted in an extended period of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations in surrounding counties reported one-quarter mile visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

Low clouds and patchy freezing drizzle combined with temperatures around 30 degrees to cause slippery roads during this Saturday morning.  Several minor vehicular accidents were reported in the Kanawha Valley. One accident lead to an indirect death in Putnam County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman lost control of her car on slippery Midday Hollow Road, between Eleanor and Buffalo around 0900E.  Her vehicle went down an embankment and struck a tree.   She was pronounced dead on the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough, developing through the central U.S. and swinging into the southeast states the Tuesday before Thanksgiving induced an area of low pressure along the eastern Gulf Coastal region.  As the upper trough shifted into the eastern U.S., the surface low underwent explosive development as it moved northward along the southeast and Mid-Atlantic coastal region.  The deepening surface low pulled cold air and moisture into the region bringing a period of snow to areas west of the Blue Ridge on one of the busiest travel days of the year.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the one to three inch range, but several locations saw snowfall in the three to four inch range across southwest Virginia, northwest North Carolina, and southeast West Virginia.  The heaviest snowfall within southeast West Virginia fell in northern Mercer county with six inches at Flat Top.  However, snowfall amounts of five inches were also observed in parts of southern Greenbrier and eastern Monroe county.||Here are some of the snow reports from southeast West Virginia as of mid-morning on the day before Thanksgiving, namely November 26, 2014.  The amounts below represent the maximum amounts reported from these counties.||Greenbrier (Organ Cave) - 5 inches,|Mercer (Flat Top) - 6 inches,|Monroe (Gap Mills) - 5 inches,|Summers (Jumping Branch) - 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McRoss CO-OP observer reported 2.0 inches of snow while the public measured 2.0 inches of snow at Williamsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.

A fast moving clipper system moved up the Ohio River Valley during the evening of the 14th.  Snow streaked east into the mid Ohio Valley counties during the evening hours.  Further south, a mixture of rain and snow changed over to all snow after 0000E on the 15th, limiting the snow accumulations.  In the wake of the weak low pressure system, colder air rushed in during the predawn hours.  The snow diminished to a few flurries during the morning in the Ohio River counties. ||Accumulations of 3 to 4 inches occurred in Wirt, Ritchie, Calhoun Counties to meet minimum warning criteria for those counties.||However, fluffy snow showers lingered into the early evening hours over the central mountain counties.  This resulted in 24 hour accumulations of 3 to 5 inches in parts of Upshur, Webster, Nicholas, Randolph, and Pocahontas Counties.  In the wake of the snowy pattern since the 12th, the maximum snow depths in these central mountains remained around a foot and a half.

A deep upper trough, developing through the central U.S. and swinging into the southeast states the Tuesday before Thanksgiving induced an area of low pressure along the eastern Gulf Coastal region.  As the upper trough shifted into the eastern U.S., the surface low underwent explosive development as it moved northward along the southeast and Mid-Atlantic coastal region.  The deepening surface low pulled cold air and moisture into the region bringing a period of snow to areas west of the Blue Ridge on one of the busiest travel days of the year.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the one to three inch range, but several locations saw snowfall in the three to four inch range across southwest Virginia, northwest North Carolina, and southeast West Virginia.  The heaviest snowfall within southeast West Virginia fell in northern Mercer county with six inches at Flat Top.  However, snowfall amounts of five inches were also observed in parts of southern Greenbrier and eastern Monroe county.||Here are some of the snow reports from southeast West Virginia as of mid-morning on the day before Thanksgiving, namely November 26, 2014.  The amounts below represent the maximum amounts reported from these counties.||Greenbrier (Organ Cave) - 5 inches,|Mercer (Flat Top) - 6 inches,|Monroe (Gap Mills) - 5 inches,|Summers (Jumping Branch) - 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed 5.0 inches of snow at Organ Cave, just south of Lewisburg.  Elsewhere across eastern Greenbrier county, snowfall amounts ranged from 1.0 inch at Renick, Lewisburg, and White Sulphur Springs, to 3.0 inches at Friars Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.

A fast moving clipper system moved up the Ohio River Valley during the evening of the 14th.  Snow streaked east into the mid Ohio Valley counties during the evening hours.  Further south, a mixture of rain and snow changed over to all snow after 0000E on the 15th, limiting the snow accumulations.  In the wake of the weak low pressure system, colder air rushed in during the predawn hours.  The snow diminished to a few flurries during the morning in the Ohio River counties. ||Accumulations of 3 to 4 inches occurred in Wirt, Ritchie, Calhoun Counties to meet minimum warning criteria for those counties.||However, fluffy snow showers lingered into the early evening hours over the central mountain counties.  This resulted in 24 hour accumulations of 3 to 5 inches in parts of Upshur, Webster, Nicholas, Randolph, and Pocahontas Counties.  In the wake of the snowy pattern since the 12th, the maximum snow depths in these central mountains remained around a foot and a half.

An arctic cold front sweep through West Virginia between midnight and 0400E on Monday the 6th.  Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were common with the front.  Rain showers and temperatures in the 40s and 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by dawn.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down into the single digits by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th across the lowlands were mostly 2 below zero to 8 below zero.  The coldest temperatures came from the highest elevations.  Readings around 10 below were common across the mountains.  The coldest readings came from the highest reporting station.  Preliminary reports indicated that Snowshoe was 21 degrees below zero for the coldest.   For many counties it was the coldest temperature since early February back in 1996.  Also, for many southern counties including around Huntington, Charleston, Williamson, Logan, and even Beckley, it was the first sub zero readings since the winter of 2008 to 2009. ||Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 30 degree range overnight and into the morning hours for most counties.  Of course, wind chill readings were even lower over the exposed highest mountain ridges.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to recover during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the mid and late afternoon.  ||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  The frozen pipes occurred in homes, schools, and even public utility supply lines under roads.  Marshall University had its share of water leaks, especially in their science building.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public school systems were closed.  A few county school districts were closed an extra day due to lingering power outages or water damage from the broken pipes.  The Wood County school system had trouble keeping their bus engines running.  The bio-diesel fuel was turning to gel, causing the engines to stall.

A low pressure system tracked east, across Kentucky into southern West Virginia during the daylight hours on the 2nd.  A mixture of light rain and snow fell during the day.  The initial precipitation caused some pockets of freezing rain in the southern coal fields counties of West Virginia near dawn. Numerous vehicular accidents occurred due to light icing from patches of freezing rain in Logan and Boone Counties.  For example, the Boone County 911 center reported 15 to 20 vehicular accidents.||The light rain and snow changed to all snow toward sunset. ||In the wake of the low center, arctic air poured south causing a quick freeze and lingering snow showers into the night.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 7 inches were common in many mountain counties, with less in the western lowlands.||Temperatures plummeted into the single digits and lower teens for most of the lowlands with some below zero reading above 3000 feet in elevation.

Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.

A passing upper level disturbance triggered light rain across the Mid-Atlantic. High pressure building south from New England resulted in a wedge of cold air, keeping surface temperatures at or just below freezing mainly at higher elevations, which resulted in areas of light ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icing was reported on roadways in surrounding counties with mesonets reporting temperatures at or just below freezing and light rain was on going.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

An arctic cold front sweep through West Virginia between midnight and 0400E on Monday the 6th.  Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were common with the front.  Rain showers and temperatures in the 40s and 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by dawn.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down into the single digits by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th across the lowlands were mostly 2 below zero to 8 below zero.  The coldest temperatures came from the highest elevations.  Readings around 10 below were common across the mountains.  The coldest readings came from the highest reporting station.  Preliminary reports indicated that Snowshoe was 21 degrees below zero for the coldest.   For many counties it was the coldest temperature since early February back in 1996.  Also, for many southern counties including around Huntington, Charleston, Williamson, Logan, and even Beckley, it was the first sub zero readings since the winter of 2008 to 2009. ||Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 30 degree range overnight and into the morning hours for most counties.  Of course, wind chill readings were even lower over the exposed highest mountain ridges.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to recover during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the mid and late afternoon.  ||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  The frozen pipes occurred in homes, schools, and even public utility supply lines under roads.  Marshall University had its share of water leaks, especially in their science building.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public school systems were closed.  A few county school districts were closed an extra day due to lingering power outages or water damage from the broken pipes.  The Wood County school system had trouble keeping their bus engines running.  The bio-diesel fuel was turning to gel, causing the engines to stall.

Rain showers and temperatures in the 40s and 50s quickly became snow showers with temperatures falling through the 20s by dawn.  Snow accumulations were less than 2 inches.  Temperatures continued to fall during the day on the 6th, with blustery winds.  Readings reached down into the single digits by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th across the lowlands were mostly 2 below zero to 8 below zero.  The coldest temperatures came from the highest elevations.  Readings around 10 below were common across the mountains.  The coldest readings came from the highest reporting station.  Preliminary reports indicated that Snowshoe was 21 degrees below zero for the coldest.   For many counties it was the coldest temperature since early February back in 1996.  Also, for many southern counties including around Huntington, Charleston, Williamson, Logan, and even Beckley, it was the first sub zero readings since the winter of 2008 to 2009. ||Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 30 degree range overnight and into the morning hours for most counties.  Of course, wind chill readings were even lower over the exposed highest mountain ridges.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to recover during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the mid and late afternoon.  ||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  The frozen pipes occurred in homes, schools, and even public utility supply lines under roads.  Marshall University had its share of water leaks, especially in their science building.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public school systems were closed.  A few county school districts were closed an extra day due to lingering power outages or water damage from the broken pipes.  The Wood County school system had trouble keeping their bus engines running.  The bio-diesel fuel was turning to gel, causing the engines to stall.

Another shot of arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

A low pressure system tracked east, across Kentucky into southern West Virginia during the daylight hours on the 2nd.  A mixture of light rain and snow fell during the day.  The initial precipitation caused some pockets of freezing rain in the southern coal fields counties of West Virginia near dawn. Numerous vehicular accidents occurred due to light icing from patches of freezing rain in Logan and Boone Counties.  For example, the Boone County 911 center reported 15 to 20 vehicular accidents.||The light rain and snow changed to all snow toward sunset. ||In the wake of the low center, arctic air poured south causing a quick freeze and lingering snow showers into the night.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 7 inches were common in many mountain counties, with less in the western lowlands.||Temperatures plummeted into the single digits and lower teens for most of the lowlands with some below zero reading above 3000 feet in elevation.

Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.

A fast moving clipper system moved up the Ohio River Valley during the evening of the 14th.  Snow streaked east into the mid Ohio Valley counties during the evening hours.  Further south, a mixture of rain and snow changed over to all snow after 0000E on the 15th, limiting the snow accumulations.  In the wake of the weak low pressure system, colder air rushed in during the predawn hours.  The snow diminished to a few flurries during the morning in the Ohio River counties. ||Accumulations of 3 to 4 inches occurred in Wirt, Ritchie, Calhoun Counties to meet minimum warning criteria for those counties.||However, fluffy snow showers lingered into the early evening hours over the central mountain counties.  This resulted in 24 hour accumulations of 3 to 5 inches in parts of Upshur, Webster, Nicholas, Randolph, and Pocahontas Counties.  In the wake of the snowy pattern since the 12th, the maximum snow depths in these central mountains remained around a foot and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation as high pressure retreated to the north of the area and low pressure formed over the Mid-Atlantic region. The northeast flow kept temperatures right around freezing during the evening and overnight hours while the forming low brought plenty of moisture, resulting in on-going precipitation for most of the night and into the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of ice accumulations between a hundredth and two-tenths were reported in surrounding counties. In addition, multiple reports of snow around 3 inches were also reported.

Readings reached down into the single digits by sunset.  ||Temperatures at dawn on the 7th across the lowlands were mostly 2 below zero to 8 below zero.  The coldest temperatures came from the highest elevations.  Readings around 10 below were common across the mountains.  The coldest readings came from the highest reporting station.  Preliminary reports indicated that Snowshoe was 21 degrees below zero for the coldest.   For many counties it was the coldest temperature since early February back in 1996.  Also, for many southern counties including around Huntington, Charleston, Williamson, Logan, and even Beckley, it was the first sub zero readings since the winter of 2008 to 2009. ||Wind chill readings bottomed out in the minus 20 to minus 30 degree range overnight and into the morning hours for most counties.  Of course, wind chill readings were even lower over the exposed highest mountain ridges.||Despite sunshine, temperatures were slow to recover during the day on the 7th.  However, the wind did subside during the mid and late afternoon.  ||A scattering of frozen pipes, power outages, furnace difficulties, and vehicular engine problems occurred during the cold wave.  The frozen pipes occurred in homes, schools, and even public utility supply lines under roads.  Marshall University had its share of water leaks, especially in their science building.  Repair companies were kept busy.  County public school systems were closed.  A few county school districts were closed an extra day due to lingering power outages or water damage from the broken pipes.  The Wood County school system had trouble keeping their bus engines running.  The bio-diesel fuel was turning to gel, causing the engines to stall.

A low pressure system tracked east, across Kentucky into southern West Virginia during the daylight hours on the 2nd.  A mixture of light rain and snow fell during the day.  The initial precipitation caused some pockets of freezing rain in the southern coal fields counties of West Virginia near dawn. Numerous vehicular accidents occurred due to light icing from patches of freezing rain in Logan and Boone Counties.  For example, the Boone County 911 center reported 15 to 20 vehicular accidents.||The light rain and snow changed to all snow toward sunset. ||In the wake of the low center, arctic air poured south causing a quick freeze and lingering snow showers into the night.  Snow accumulations of 3 to 7 inches were common in many mountain counties, with less in the western lowlands.||Temperatures plummeted into the single digits and lower teens for most of the lowlands with some below zero reading above 3000 feet in elevation.

Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.

Coastal low pressure system brought snow to areas south and east of Pittsburgh during the day on the 26th. 6-8 inches of snow was reported across Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia and Garrett county in Maryland.

Cold air damming situation evolved Tuesday as a cold front stalled just to the south of the Mid-Atlantic region and high pressure built in over New England. This synoptic set up resulted in persisting northeasterly flow that kept surface temperatures at or just below freezing while southwesterly flow in the lower levels provided a deep warm layer. This combination of temperatures with the overrunning precipitation resulted in ice accumulations across most of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of between a trace and 0.2 inches of ice were reported in surrounding counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance triggered light rain across the Mid-Atlantic. High pressure building south from New England resulted in a wedge of cold air, keeping surface temperatures at or just below freezing mainly at higher elevations, which resulted in areas of light ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department in Hartmansville reported icing on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation as high pressure retreated to the north of the area and low pressure formed over the Mid-Atlantic region. The northeast flow kept temperatures right around freezing during the evening and overnight hours while the forming low brought plenty of moisture, resulting in on-going precipitation for most of the night and into the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of ice accumulations between a hundredth and two-tenths were reported in surrounding counties. In addition, multiple reports of snow around 3 inches were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of ice accumulations between a hundredth and two-tenths were reported in surrounding counties. In addition, multiple reports of snow between 3 and 4 inches were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 27th.  A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

A very high amplitude upper atmospheric pattern featured equally deep troughs in the western and eastern U.S.  Within the eastern U.S. trough, was a vigorous Alberta clipper that intensified immensely as it plunged into the southeast states into the base of the upper trough.  Meanwhile...a large nearly 1040mb Canadian High was plunging into the central and eastern U.S. on the back side of the developing storm system in the southeast states.  The combination of these features brought an early season snowfall principally to the Appalachian mountains of eastern Tennessee, far southwest Virginia, and northwest North Carolina.  Light snow also fell in the mountains of eastern West Virginia, but the storm system tracked too far south for any significant amounts in that region.  Snowfall amounts were generally around 1 inch or less, except 1 to 3 inch range across this region...with some higher totals of 4 to 6 inches in the higher elevations of northwest North Carolina.  Strong and gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow in these areas causing scattered power outages.  Because of warm temperatures within the preceding days and the early season time of the event, roads and travel conditions were not significantly impacted.||Here are some of the snow reports from southeast West Virginia from the afternoon of November 1, 2014.  These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Greenbrier (McRoss) - 1.1 inch,|Mercer (Bluefield) - 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McRoss COOP observer reported 1.1 inch of snow while the West Virginia Department of Highways observed 1.0 inch of snow at Quinwood.  Elsewhere in western Greenbrier county, snow amounts were less than one inch.

These denote the high end of the event in these counties.  Note, the event actually began during the afternoon of October 31st.||Greenbrier (McRoss) - 1.1 inch,|Mercer (Bluefield) - 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed 3.0 inches of snow in Bluefield.  Elsewhere around the county, snow amounts ranged from 1/2 inch at Princeton to 1.1 inch at the Mercer County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong warm air advection in the Ohio Valley helped snow to develop just before dawn in the Ohio River counties. The snow spread quickly east.  Lulls in the snowfall developed during the midday.  A strong cold front and its associated mid level disturbance helped trigger more showery snow during the early evening.  Lightning and thunder was even observed, causing white out conditions for about 30 minutes. ||Snow totals of 4 to 7 inches fell across Roane and Braxton Counties, where the convection bands trained a bit longer.  Meanwhile, snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were more common in southern West Virginia, with 3 to 5 inches across the northern lowlands and the central mountain counties.
High pressure building in from the west coupled with a deepening upper level trough ushered in record breaking cold temperatures for multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of temperatures and winds resulted in a wind chill of at least -10oF during the early morning hours on the 18th and 19th.

A clear night with diminishing winds resulted in temperatures well below zero over northern counties to around zero in the southern counties.||The coldest temperatures at dawn on the 28th from the official cooperative network was 13 degrees below zero from Snowshoe.  Middlebourne and Sandyville observed minus 12.  Harrisville saw its temperature drop to 11 below zero, while West Union observed minus 9.  Rock Cave reported a minimum temperature of minus 7.  Readings of minus 6 and minus 7 were seen around Elkins and Glady.  ||Dawn on the 29th was another very cold morning. Temperatures were colder in southern counties than the previous dawn.  Preliminary data indicated the coldest temperature from the official cooperative observers network was 18 below zero from northeast of Sandyville of northern Jackson County.  Middleboure of Tyler County and Mt Nebo of Nicholas County were not  far behind with 16 below zero. West Union observed 14 below zero,  Rosedale of Braxton County had minus 13, while Elkins, Buckhannon, and Spencer had minus 12.  The cooperative observers at Clarksburg, Sissonville, and Grantsville all observed minus 10.  Glenville and Pineville had minus 9.  Charleston reached down to minus 5, the coldest reading there since February of 1996.  Oceana of Wyoming County also observed 5 below zero.| |Some unofficial readings included, minus 11 at Jane Lew, minus 10 at Lakin in Mason County, and minus 7 at Beech Fork in Wayne County.||Dawn on the 30th was another cold morning, with the coldest readings finally shifting into the deep mountain valleys. Glady reported 18 degrees below zero.  Marlinton observed 15 below zero, while Elkins had minus 11.  An unofficial reading of 17 below zero was measured at Green Bank.||The 3 day cold wave finally ended as temperatures moderated during the sunny afternoon of Thursday the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air damming situation evolved Tuesday as a cold front stalled just to the south of the Mid-Atlantic region and high pressure built in over New England. This synoptic set up resulted in persisting northeasterly flow that kept surface temperatures at or just below freezing while southwesterly flow in the lower levels provided a deep warm layer. This combination of temperatures with the overrunning precipitation resulted in ice accumulations across most of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of between a trace and 0.2 inches of ice were reported in surrounding counties.

Light to moderate rain fell most of the day.  Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SUV traveling northbound on Interstate 77 lost control on wet pavement and veered into the median.  This occurred at mile marker 143 between Ripley and Ravenswood. The vehicle crashed through a cable median and into the southbound traffic.  A pickup truck traveling south hit the broadside of the SUV causing it to catch fire.  Five of the 6 people in the SUV were killed. These are considered indirect weather deaths.  ||Investigating officers of the WV State Police believed weather played a contributing factor.  Areas of moderate rain were in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was located across the Mid-Atlantic. Calm winds and clear skies led to dense fog formation across valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities of a quarter mile or less was measured at surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer air aloft moving over cold low levels produced a mix of sleet and freezing rain from the evening of the 1st into the early morning hours of the 3rd. A light coating of ice was reported with some accidents on untreated roads due to slick conditions across Garrett county Maryland, eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.

A tight pressure gradient with a powerful cold front produced wind gusts over 45 MPH in locations across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia the evening of December 24th. Highest reported winds in Pennsylvania were 56 MPH in Du Bois, 55 MPH in Pittsburgh, 54 MPH at Latrobe, and 45 MPH near Mcmurray in Washington county. Highest winds in Ohio were 55 MPH at Zanesville, 52 MPH in Coshocton, and 45 MPH in Hanoverton in Columbiana county. Winds gusted to 55 MPH in Wheeling, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 55 MPH was measured at the Wheeling airport at 741 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure resided across the east coast while a warm front was located across the Carolinas. Moist air continued to move into the Mid Atlantic as showers intensified ahead of the warm front. Heavy rain produced flooding in parts of the Washington DC and Baltimore Metro Areas. Thunderstorms produced damaging winds west of the Blue Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a large tree down blocking Grassy Lick Road about one half mile from Route 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance combining with northwest winds triggered upslope snow showers on the western side of the Potomac Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported a 12 hour storm snowfall total of 3.0 inches near Bayard. A public report from Mt. Storm also reported 3.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave crossing the upper Ohio Valley brought light freezing rain through the morning hours of the 22nd. While strong warm advection was ongoing ahead of the system aloft...a strong inversion at the surface did not allow for temperatures to warm above freezing at the onset precipitation. This allowed for a light glazing of ice on untreated surfaces over portions of eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and counties north of I-70 in Pennsylvania. Several accidents were reported across the advisory area, including a head-on collision between a car and a school bus in Columbiana county, Ohio.

Coastal low pressure system brought snow to areas south and east of Pittsburgh during the day on the 26th. 6-8 inches of snow was reported across Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia and Garrett county in Maryland.
A passing upper level disturbance combining with northwest winds triggered upslope snow showers on the western side of the Potomac Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported a 12 hour storm snowfall total of 4.0 inches near Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland the morning of the 27th. Visibilities were reduced to near zero at times in snow showers along the front. As high pressure moved over the region the 28th and 29th, morning low temperatures ranged from 5 below zero to 25 below zero across the region. Light winds did not allow for extreme wind chills, with the exception being wind chills near 30 below zero across parts of Garrett and Tucker counties just after midnight on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in Weirton were -10 on the 28th and -12 on the 29th.
