EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of arctic air arrived into the mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of January 6th. Temperatures continued to fall over the next 24 hours, eventually bottoming out below zero nearly everywhere west of the Blue Ridge.  Winds remained gusty through that 24 hour period as well due to a tightened pressure gradient as high pressure built into the region. The result was dangerously low wind chill temperatures not see in the region in many years.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures were observed in the -20F to -30F range at several locations throughout Mercer County during the early morning hours of January 7th, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports ranging from five to six inches of snow accumulation were received from across Greenbrier County.  In addition, wind gusts as high as 51 mph were observed, resulting in blowing snow that significantly reduced visibilities, as well as dangerously low wind chill temperatures, ranging from -5F to -10F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved along an arctic boundary slowly pushing south across the upper Ohio Valley producing light snowfall. Areas south of I-70 in western Pennsylvania, much of northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland received 3 to 6 inches of snow in a 12 to 15 hour period from the early morning of the 2nd to the early morning of the 3rd. Areas in western Pennsylvania from I-70 to just north of Pittsburgh received 2 to 4 inches.

A low pressure system moved toward the upper Ohio Valley on the 29th before transitioning to the east coast on the 30th. The system produced light snow across much eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Colder northwest flow behind the system brought additional snowfall to the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges of Fayette county in Pennsylvania, the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and Garrett county in Maryland. 24 hour snow amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches in these areas, with isolated 7 to 8 inch reports above 2000 feet.

Warm and humid conditions were across the region and showers and thunderstorms led to heavy rain. Unstable conditions led to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was estimated to have touched down at approximately 737 PM EDT in the 500 Block of Tub Run Hollow Road. Extensive tree damage occurred in this area. Several dozen trees were snapped and topped and another was uprooted near a house. The tree debris pattern was convergent in nature indicating tornadic rotation. The tornado traveled approximately 130 yards towards the north northeast along Tub Run Hollow Road where it snapped and topped additional trees on both sides of the road before lifting at approximately 738 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air out ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon over western West Virginia.  These moved east and southeast into the early evening.  In a few spots the lines of convection became parallel to the flow for a short time.  This allowed downpours to last about an hour, with rain amounts upwards of 2.5 to 3 inches.  Urbanized hills increased the runoff into the valleys below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Tygart Lake measured 2.8 inches for a 24 hour total, but most of it fell during the late afternoon and early evening in a few rounds of thunderstorms.||Small streams overflowed over roads, including in the Tygart Lake campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid (dew points upper 60s) and unstable air mass allowed the development of of scattered thunderstorms, one of which became severe during the late evening in the Rainelle area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a weak cold front, thunderstorms moved south during the evening hours on the 27th, then slowly weakening in the southern coal field early on the 28th.  Local downpours with 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in an hour fell in parts of Jackson, Cabell, Putnam, Lincoln, Kanawha, Boone, Logan, and Mingo Counties.  The usual street and poor drainage flooding occurred. ||Lightning strikes from these storms did cause some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The building housing the Lincoln County 911 Center took a lightning hit.  Personnel think the lightning hit their radio tower then ran across the roof of the building. A dispatcher described the sound as marbles being dropped on the metal roof.||The phone system and some radio equipment were damaged.  Calls to the center were transferred to Boone County for about 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An individual thunderstorm cell became stronger on the southeastern edge of a larger cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving east out of Kentucky.  A tight low level circulation was detected by weather radar over Mingo County for a brief time.

A strong wind flow existed in the lower atmosphere, well ahead of a surface cold front.  Clusters of fast moving showers and thunderstorms raced across northeast Kentucky and into southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Late in the evening, a brief EF2 tornado occurred over the high terrain near Odd in southern Raleigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days with rounds of showers and thunderstorms continued on Friday the 22nd.  The area was in the active storm track, in the northwest flow aloft, from the Midwest into the central Appalachians.||Clusters showers and thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon hours and moved through southern West Virginia, especially counties in the Tug Fork and Guyandotte River Valleys.  Rains of 2 to 3 inches in 1 to 2 hours fell in the downpours.  The automatic gauge at the Tri State Airport in northern Wayne County measured 1 inch of rain in 7 minutes near 1700E.  Their total observed rain total was near 2.6 inches in about 90 minutes.  The nearby cooperative observer in the western portion of Huntington had a rain total of 2.1 inches.   Flash flooding occurred during the early evening.  ||A totally separate area of rain sank south late at night into northern West Virginia.  There were small bands of showers with concentrated downpours.  One of these bands set up near Audra State Park along the Upshur and Barbour County line.  One automatic gauge in that vicinity reported 2.8 inches of rain in an hour.  Road damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Culverts were washed out on Messinger Way off of Swamp Run Road near Audra State Park.  Residents in about 8 homes could not get out.  A culvert on Hardman Road, or County Route 3/9, was also washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid Atlantic and southerly winds increased dew points across the region. Instability increased across the region and showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern WV and western Maryland. Some thunderstorms were capable of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees down near the intersection of River Road and Pious Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front moved north across eastern Ohio and the northern West Virginia Panhandle, repeated thunderstorms caused flash flooding across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding in Wheeling with water over 4 foot deep flowing across U.S. 40 closing the road. Water was in the lower floors of some buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the northwest pushed into a typically very warm and humid air mass, with thunderstorms resulting. A few storms became severe producing pockets of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds in Pipestem Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban and rural flash flooding occurred.  Many streets in Huntington were flooded including the usual viaducts.  The water caused some road damage.  Water was 1 to 2 feet deep in spots.  Water seeped into businesses, the Marshall University library, and the basement of a hospital. A discount store on 5th Avenue lost some merchandise. An animal hospital off of Route 60 was flooded, but the dogs and cats inside were saved. On 25th Street, water was into some homes.  Several vehicles were flooded when trapped in the high water.    ||Outside of the city, small streams such as Fourpole and Grapevine Branch flooded roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong subtropical ridge of high pressure aloft was centered along the south central Gulf Coastal region.  Some of the hottest weather of the summer season was present across the area at the time with maximum temperatures into the low 90s across the Piedmont, with 80s across the mountains. An upper-level disturbance was tracking through the Great Lakes into the northeastern United States.  This disturbance impinged upon the hot, humid, unstable air mass in place across the region to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these became severe in the northwest part of the forecast area, particularly in far southeastern West Virginia and adjacent areas of southwest Virginia, producing damaging wind gusts and marginally severe hail along with frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that at least eleven trees were down across a 1/2 mile-wide area just to the east-northeast of Red Sulphur Springs.  Additional damage was noted to the southeast just northeast of Lindside, where several large tree limbs were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid (dew points upper 60s) and unstable air mass allowed the development of of scattered thunderstorms, one of which became severe during the late evening in the Rainelle area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees, street lights and power lines were brought down by the severe winds. Several power poles were snapped and some damage to a car wash occurred.

Some of the hottest weather of the summer season was present across the area at the time with maximum temperatures into the low 90s across the Piedmont, with 80s across the mountains. An upper-level disturbance was tracking through the Great Lakes into the northeastern United States.  This disturbance impinged upon the hot, humid, unstable air mass in place across the region to generate scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these became severe in the northwest part of the forecast area, particularly in far southeastern West Virginia and adjacent areas of southwest Virginia, producing damaging wind gusts and marginally severe hail along with frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WVVA Television relayed a report from the public of penny-sized hail in the Red Sulphur Springs area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a  muggy late summer air mass, several clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved through southern West Virginia from midday into the evening.  Downpours were the main result. The heaviest cells were producing .5 to .75 inches of rain in 15 minutes.  Luckily the movement of the storms was around 35 mph, so it took repetitive storms to cause runoff problems.  Maximum rain totals were estimated mostly in the 2 to 3 inch range.  However, a maximum of 3 to 3.5 inches was estimated south of Panther in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams, such as Mud Fork, Trap Fork and Greenbrier Creek, flooded roads and surrounded a few homes in the western portion of the county.   No homes were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a  muggy late summer air mass, several clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved through southern West Virginia from midday into the evening.  Downpours were the main result. The heaviest cells were producing .5 to .75 inches of rain in 15 minutes.  Luckily the movement of the storms was around 35 mph, so it took repetitive storms to cause runoff problems.  Maximum rain totals were estimated mostly in the 2 to 3 inch range.  However, a maximum of 3 to 3.5 inches was estimated south of Panther in McDowell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor drainage flooding was common along streets during the late afternoon into the early evening.  One low spot collected water about a foot deep on New Goff Mountain Road near Cross Lanes.  An ambulance stalled while trying to drive through the pool of water. ||The cooperative observer in Saint Albans measured 2.16 inches of rain for their 24 hour total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air out ahead of a cold front, showers and thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon over western West Virginia.  These moved east and southeast into the early evening.  In a few spots the lines of convection became parallel to the flow for a short time.  This allowed downpours to last about an hour, with rain amounts upwards of 2.5 to 3 inches.  Urbanized hills increased the runoff into the valleys below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yeager Airport measured 1.25 inches of rain in about 35 minutes and 1.7 inches in an hour.  The rain was heavier just to the west.  Amounts in the 2.5 inch range were estimated over the hills on the west side of Charleston to South Charleston. Yet, 1 unofficial report was 3 inches from a rain gauge on Bigley Avenue. ||The runoff overwhelmed and inundated the urban drainage system in sections of Charleston and South Charleston.  Streams such as Magazine Branch, Sugar Creek, and Woodward Branch flooded roads, underpasses, and collected around homes.  Water got into a few basements destroying some furniture.||Runoff off the hills and streets caused water to collect 1 to 2 feet deep on Washington Street on the west side of Charleston.  A plumbing supply store had 1 to 2 feet of water inside, damaging some of their supplies.  Another vacant business building had a foot of water inside.  Several vehicles stalled in the water. Water rescues were performed for stranded motorists along streets such as Chandler Drive, Greendale Drive, Garrison Avenue, 7th Avenue, Patrick Street, and Rock Lake Drive.  ||After the downpour, the pooled water took 1 to 2 hours to finally drain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance passing across southeast West Virginia and western Virginia provided the upper level support for isolated severe thunderstorm development, while low level convergence associated with an approaching cold front provided support in the low levels.  Environmental shear was weak, with the storms supported more by strong diurnal heating as afternoon temperatures rose into the low and mid 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven large trees were blown down along Route 219 between the communities of Union and Pickaway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating in a weak steering flow helped form slow moving thunderstorm cells.  Localized downpours were the result. A few spots received 2 inches of rain in less than an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating in a weak steering flow helped form slow moving thunderstorm cells.  Localized downpours were the result. A few spots received 2 inches of rain in less than an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams running along Swamp Run Road near Audra State Park caused minor flash flooding.  Water flowed under one house and flooded the crawl space.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached the region, surface winds increased significantly in advance of the front on Tuesday the 14th, and then continued very gusty behind the front Tuesday night. A large number of trees were blown down in association with these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the day, high winds brought down several trees across the county, some of which partially or completely blocked roads. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow line of showers, with a few embedded thunderstorms, accompanied a strong cold front during Christmas Eve.  Temperatures had been in the 60 to 65 degree range during the afternoon with dew points in the lower 50s.||In the fast moving showers, most wind gusts were in the 40 to 50 mph range across the western lowland counties, as far east as the Interstate 79 corridor.  In Boone County, a stronger gust likely occurred.  Around 4,600 customers temporarily lost electricity in southwestern West Virginia, including the Huntington to Charleston corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two single-wide mobile homes sustained damage from fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strengthening low pressure system lifted north through the Great Lakes into Ontario, strong winds aloft mixed down to the ground during the late morning and afternoon. Surface temperatures were still in the mid 60s to lower 70s with sunshine.  The cold air advection aloft aided in the downward momentum transfer.||Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common.  The strongest measured gust was 57 mph at Yeager Airport near Charleston. Huntington and Clarksburg both measured a 51 mph gust. The Mason County Airport recorded a 50 mph gust. ||Electrical outages occurred, mostly due to weak tree limbs falling on overhead lines.   Kanawha and Cabell Counties both had around 2,300 customers without electricity temporarily.  Around 2,100 customers in Wayne County lost electricity.  The other counties had less electric outages.  The wind also damaged a roof to a warehouse in Charleston.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped from southern Ohio across the southern Appalachian region. A couple of the storms across southeast West Virginia had enough low level rotation in them to produce in one instance a tornado in Mercer County, and one with damaging winds in Summers County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew ten trees and one power pole down near the Highway 20 bridge that crosses Bluestone Lake. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped from southern Ohio across the southern Appalachian region. A couple of the storms across southeast West Virginia had enough low level rotation in them to produce in one instance a tornado in Mercer County, and one with damaging winds in Summers County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high end EF1 tornado, with maximum winds estimated at 105 mph, first touched down at 1139 PM EDT around two miles northeast of Matoka in a forested area, downing numerous trees. It then crossed Beeson Road where three homes sustained minor damage along with a barn and several more trees. Additional trees were then snapped off as the tornado initially crossed Little Rich Creek Road and then proceeded to cause severe damage to a mobile home at another location along Rich Creek Road. A mother and her son sustained non-life threatening injuries at the mobile home location. More trees were then uprooted along Rich Creek. Finally, as the tornado crossed Sharver Road, minor damage occurred to a house and a small shed before the tornado lifted prior to reaching Beckley Road. Damage values are estimated.

A strong wind flow existed in the lower atmosphere, well ahead of a surface cold front.  Clusters of fast moving showers and thunderstorms raced across northeast Kentucky and into southern West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Late in the evening, a brief EF2 tornado occurred over the high terrain near Odd in southern Raleigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF2 tornado touched down near the community of Odd. This path is across terrain with an elevation of 2700 to 2800 feet and is just north of Flat Top Mountain.  ||The maximum wind gust estimated by the survey was 125 mph.||On Route 33 a well built garage or outbuilding, with a bolted foundation, was completely destroyed.  An entire couch from that building was blown across the street and wrapped around a tree.  About 10 to 20 houses, including mobile homes, had minor roof and porch damage.  A few barns and outbuildings were damaged.  At least 2 vehicles were destroyed.  A new pickup truck was smashed by a fallen tree.  Another car was hit by fallen trees. One parked school bus had its front windshield broken.   ||Hundreds of large healthy trees were blown down.  Exposed trees were blown down or uprooted.   Trees more protected by the terrain had their tops sheared off.  Power outages to several hundred people occurred.  Roads were closed by the fallen trees.  The local telephone company had around 20 poles damaged.

A developing storm along the South Carolina coast spread snow into the mountain counties of West Virginia during the predawn hours on the 26th.  The snow was the heaviest during the daylight morning hours.  The snow ended by late afternoon, but some snow showers lingered over the high mountains. ||Six to 12 inches of snow accumulated over Pocahontas and eastern portions of Randolph County, east of the Tygart River Valley.  Bartow in northern Pocahontas County had an 11 inch accumulation before settling.  Snowshoe reported accumulations of 8 to 12 inches in their vicinity.  Some unofficial measurements included 7 inches from Harman, 9 inches from Alpena, and 12 inches from Whitmer.  ||The heavy wet snow did cause widespread electric outages in Pocahontas County. At the peak of the outages during the early afternoon, nearly 6,000 customers were without electricity. The emergency manager estimated the number at around 70 percent of the county.  About 10 residents took advantage of a warming shelter that the county set up in Marlinton.  During the storm, a small fire truck with the Whitmer Volunteer Department overturned into a field.

During the afternoon of the 28th, near a stationary front, thunderstorms moved across southeast Kentucky.  They continued into far western Virginia and finally into extreme southern West Virginia.  Large hail briefly fell in McDowell County.||Additional rounds of showers and thunderstorms fell overnight and during the morning on the 29th.  Close to the stationary frontal boundary, and near the warmer and more unstable air in Kentucky, stronger and more discrete thunderstorm cells formed during the late afternoon on the 29th over eastern Kentucky and southwestern West Virginia.   Those storms moved north and remained mostly below severe limits in West Virginia. However, there was one wind damage report in Wayne County.  Localized downpours caused some minor flash flooding in western Putnam County and southeastern Cabell County.  ||As the southeast low level flow over the mountains kept the front nearly stationary, another round of convection formed after sunset in the Tri State near Huntington on the 29th.||More rounds of showers fell along the Ohio River Counties on the 30th.  Yet, rain amounts were lighter with no flooding.  Storm total rain amounts were mostly around 2 inches on the West Virginia side of the Ohio River, except 3 to 4 inches in extreme western Wayne County.  This was over a 3 day period.  For example, the Huntington airport measured 3.82 inches of rain for the episode.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gusts of 61 mph were measured at Keyser.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of five inches were reported at Bloomery.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 or more inches were measured at Berkeley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved quickly east during the late afternoon on the 3rd.  A nearly stationary west to east front was draped across northern Kentucky into West Virginia.  With more heat and instability located over eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia, the southern end of this band of convection was stronger, compared to further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across Interstate 64 and Goff Mountain Road.  The roads were temporarily closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved quickly east during the late afternoon on the 3rd.  A nearly stationary west to east front was draped across northern Kentucky into West Virginia.  With more heat and instability located over eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia, the southern end of this band of convection was stronger, compared to further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying center of low pressure passed north of the area and the trailing cold front was accompanied by very strong winds behind it as very cold air surged southward along with a large pressure rises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a dozen trees and at least three power lines were brought down by high winds. Bluefield ASOS (KBLF) had a peak wind gust of 49 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying center of low pressure passed north of the area and the trailing cold front was accompanied by very strong winds behind it as very cold air surged southward along with a large pressure rises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts around the county ranged from 56 at Lewisburg Airport ASOS (KLWB) to 58 at a mesonet site 2 NW of Alvon. One tree was reported down in Lewisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying center of low pressure passed north of the area and the trailing cold front was accompanied by very strong winds behind it as very cold air surged southward along with a large pressure rises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down by high winds on route 3, a few miles east of Hinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure tracked along a frontal boundary dropping slowly south into southern West Virginia produced periods of snow and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches with most of the county receiving close to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved quickly east during the late afternoon on the 3rd.  A nearly stationary west to east front was draped across northern Kentucky into West Virginia.  With more heat and instability located over eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia, the southern end of this band of convection was stronger, compared to further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house on Kay Neva Lane had siding blown off.  A few other houses in the vicinity had a few shingles ripped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms formed during the mid afternoon in central Ohio and dropped southeast, reaching western West Virginia by late afternoon. This was well in advance of a cold front in the Midwest.

After some morning rain showers, scattered thunderstorms formed in eastern and southern Ohio during the mid afternoon hours on the 3rd, ahead of a cold front.  The storms moved southeast into West Virginia during the evening hours.||The front sagged south to near the Interstate 64 corridor, near Huntington and Charleston, by dawn on the 4th.  A strong low pressure for early June moved east through the Midwest, along the frontal boundary, during the day on the 4th.  Dew points were in the upper 60s and lower 70s near and south of the front.  The disturbance passed to the east by early on the 5th.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms passed through West Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours on the 4th.  The last of the showers and thunderstorms exited McDowell County during the predawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.  A few tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms formed during the midday in Ohio, well in advance of a cold front in the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes. These storms raced east into central and northern West Virginia at 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  Their energy was derived mostly by those winds aloft. Brief severe wind gusts reached the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a slow moving mid level trough in the Missouri, warm and humid air returned to northeast Kentucky on the 10th.  Sunshine helped temperatures reach into the mid 80s during the afternoon.  An arch of thunderstorms moved northeast out of Tennessee during the late afternoon, through eastern Kentucky and into West Virginia near sunset.  A well defined shelf cloud was observed on the leading edge of the squall line.  Wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph were widespread.  For example, the Tri State Airport near Huntington had a gust to 51 mph at 1909E, a spotter near Leon had a 45 mph gust around 1955E, and Charleston had a 46 mph gust near 2030E.  A few spots received slightly stronger wind gusts.  Electrical power outages occurred.  Cabell County and the Huntington vicinity had about 16,000 customers without electricity.       ||After an overnight and morning lull, more rounds of convection developed during midday on the 11th in southwest Virginia and southern West Virginia.  Those showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through portions of West Virginia during the afternoon.||Additional clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through West Virginia during the overnight hours of the 11th into the 12th.  Some of these showers were elongated along their movement.  Local rains of 1.5 to 2 inches over wet terrain managed to caused minor flash flooding.||The final round of showers and thunderstorms came during the afternoon and early evening of the 13th, as the surface cold front was moving through.  Some minor urban and poor drainage flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving across the region in the afternoon helped to produce showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe. In addition weak upper level flow allowed for several storms to produce heavy rainfall. The heavy rain caused flooding to several portions of Westmoreland county, PA including Latrobe.

After some morning rain showers, scattered thunderstorms formed in eastern and southern Ohio during the mid afternoon hours on the 3rd, ahead of a cold front.  The storms moved southeast into West Virginia during the evening hours.||The front sagged south to near the Interstate 64 corridor, near Huntington and Charleston, by dawn on the 4th.  A strong low pressure for early June moved east through the Midwest, along the frontal boundary, during the day on the 4th.  Dew points were in the upper 60s and lower 70s near and south of the front.  The disturbance passed to the east by early on the 5th.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms passed through West Virginia during the afternoon and evening hours on the 4th.  The last of the showers and thunderstorms exited McDowell County during the predawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated rain amounts of 2 to 3.5 inches in less than 3 hours over a small portion of the county along the Virginia border.  A gauge south of the heavy axis at Jackson Flats measured around 2.2 inches.||Flash flooding occurred along the deep narrow valleys comprising both forks of Bull Creek.  At the headwaters of the Left Fork of Bull Creek, the Mud Fork and the Road Fork also flooded.  The feeder streams into Panther Creek called Greenbrier Fork and Trap Fork also flooded.  The terrain varies about 800 feet between the ridge tops and hollows.  One sleeping resident was warned by a phone call.  A neighbor told her, your car is floating in your driveway.  Another resident said, I've been here 25 years, and this is about the worst we've ever had.    ||Preliminary damage assessment had 1 house destroyed. Two other houses had minor damage and 49 others were affected. Two restaurants had minor damage. Fifty small private bridges and 20 vehicles were damaged.  Personnel from the West Virginia National Guard were used to gain access to remote areas.  The adjacent roads to these streams had sections washed out or undermined.||Localized flash flooding also occurred across the state border in Buchanan County, Virginia.

Strong southerly flow and a surface low riding along a stalled cold front over Ohio, provided for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening on May 14th. With strengthening shear, several storms maintained strong updrafts, producing quarter to baseball sized hail over Ohio and the northern West Virginia panhandle. In addition, heavy rain of up to 3 inches fell over Preston and Tucker counties, causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down along Buffalo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary ran from Kentucky east into southern West Virginia on the 8th into the 9th.  Warm and muggy air was south of the front, while drier air was to the north and northeast.  One round of showers moved across southern West Virginia during the afternoon and early evening of the 8th.  Rains of 1 to 2 inches were common in Wyoming, eastern McDowell, and southern Raleigh Counties.  Another cluster of efficient showers moved through during the mid morning into the early afternoon of the 9th.  Another 1.5 to 3 inches of rain fell.  Lighter showers lingered over a small area into the late afternoon and early evening.   One gauge near Odd reported a 24 hour total around 5 inches.  Small streams rose, but due to the initial dry conditions, only a few spots saw minor flash flooding on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The headwaters of Elkhorn Creek flooded roads around Maybeury and Switchback.  Roads such as Powerhouse Hill, Route 52/38, and Route 901/56 were closed.  Roads were undermined in a few spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid Atlantic and southerly winds increased dew points across the region. Instability increased across the region and showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern WV and western Maryland. Some thunderstorms were capable of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was estimated to have touched down around 841 PM EDT near the 1100 Block of Carolina Street, where it lifted off a 10 foot by 20 food section of front porch roof of a residence. This roof panel came to rest on the top of the same house. The tornado continued moving east producing scattered tree damage.  Two trees fell on a house near Porter Street and South Kentucky Avenue. More tree damage, large limbs from topped trees was found in an area bounded by Winchester Ave, South Raleigh Street and Silver Lane and East South Street.||After crossing South Raleigh Street, the most concentrated storm damage was found. Dozens of trees were either topped or snapped on the grounds of Historic Boydville Manor in the 600 Block South Queen Street. A large HVAC panel from a nearby business was found embedded in the ground behind a cottage located on the grounds of the manor. Two eyewitness accounts there confirmed hearing a loud freight train noise at the time of the damage and reported high winds blowing from different directions. At these locations, peak winds were estimated at 90 mph and the tornado was its widest at approximately 100 yards.||After crossing South Queen Street, several large trees were topped between Queen Street and the railroad tracks and along either side of East Addition Street. From this point no further damage east of the railroad tracks was observed by the survey team. The tornado was estimated to have quickly lifted around 844 PM EDT...a total of three minutes on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well ahead of a strong cold front in Indiana, one cluster of thunderstorms moved southeast through Ohio during the afternoon, reaching northern West Virginia during the evening hours.  With a lot of wind energy above the ground, some storm cells pulsed to severe limits despite nightfall.   Storm surveys concluded 2 brief tornadoes touched down.  Brief downpours occurred too, with some measured rain amounts of an inch in less the 30 minutes. ||Other storms formed in Indiana and reached western West Virginia near sunset.  These had considerable lightning as they crossed the southern half of the state, but remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down along Route 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was moving through the central Ohio Valley the afternoon the the 8th. Showers and storms developed in association with it.  One storm was severe and produced a microburst that downed hundreds of trees over a length of 1000 yards. Another storm produced very heavy rain that caused mud to inundate a road near Blue Bend. Damage values are estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local emergency management survey indicated a couple hundred trees were blown down over a length of 1000 yards by a suspected microburst in the Blue Bend area along Anthony Road, roughly two miles west of the intersection of Anthony Road and WV92. Most trees were uprooted, and some were snapped. Many of the trees fell in a campgrounds area where signage, trash cans, and one setup camp were damaged. Damage values are estimated.

Local volunteer fire department personnel helped residents that had their homes surrounded by high water.

Brief downpours occurred too, with some measured rain amounts of an inch in less the 30 minutes. ||Other storms formed in Indiana and reached western West Virginia near sunset.  These had considerable lightning as they crossed the southern half of the state, but remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down along Interstate 64 and MacCorkle Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the wind flow aloft, along with afternoon heating and instability, helped maintain thunderstorms. They moved through extreme southeast Ohio into West Virginia.  The most power outages occurred in Cabell County with over 7,000 customers affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down around the intersection of McComas and Martha Roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree along Garden Terrace.  The tree fell onto power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred along the Left Fork of French Creek and County Route 20 in eastern Pleasants County.  The rating was based on the damage to healthy hardwood trees, with peak wind gusts near 100 mph.  Many trees were snapped or uprooted.  One small metal outbuilding was destroyed.  Thankfully, there were no fatalities or injuries. ||The path of this tornado continued past Old State Road or County Route 3/1 into Ritchie County.

The maximum gusts were near 100 mph in Ritchie County too. The path ended near Route 16.  The total path length in both counties was just over 4 miles.||A single family house had damage to windows and roofing.  Large trees were snapped.  Thankfully, there were no fatalities or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well ahead of a strong cold front in Indiana, one cluster of thunderstorms moved southeast through Ohio during the afternoon, reaching northern West Virginia during the evening hours.  With a lot of wind energy above the ground, some storm cells pulsed to severe limits despite nightfall.   Storm surveys concluded 2 brief tornadoes touched down.  Brief downpours occurred too, with some measured rain amounts of an inch in less the 30 minutes. ||Other storms formed in Indiana and reached western West Virginia near sunset.  These had considerable lightning as they crossed the southern half of the state, but remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on a ridge along Chapman Lane.  This is off of County Route 48 southeast of Lost Creek.  Numerous trees were downed.  The tornado continued across County Route 52 where additional trees and power lines were blown down.  At this point, the tornado struck the Johnstown United Methodist Church.  The church was of brick construction, originally from 1884.  Modifications have been made to the structure over the years.  It is believed the roof was lifted from the church and then fell back into place.  In doing so, it caused structural damage to the back brick wall.  The church steeple was also damaged.  Even some tombstones in the cemetery were blown over and damaged by fallen debris. Several large trees were blown down around the church.  Some of those trees were over 100 years old. The tornado lifted near the next ridge.||The strongest wind gusts were estimated near 100 mph based on the healthy trees that were snapped and uprooted, plus the structural damage to the church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the wind flow aloft, along with afternoon heating and instability, helped maintain thunderstorms. They moved through extreme southeast Ohio into West Virginia.  The most power outages occurred in Cabell County with over 7,000 customers affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch fell onto the bed of a pickup truck near the intersection of 9th Avenue and 24th Street.  Other trees fell within the city including along 8th Avenue.  Some power lines were brought down too.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the wind flow aloft, along with afternoon heating and instability, helped maintain thunderstorms. They moved through extreme southeast Ohio into West Virginia.  The most power outages occurred in Cabell County with over 7,000 customers affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains estimated at 1 to 2 inches fell in less than 2 hours.  Minor flash flooding occurred along small creeks west of the Interstate 79 corridor.  Water flowed over roads.  One example was northeast of Camden, where the Dry Fork flooded near the intersection with Rush Run Road.  No structures were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the region in advance of an approaching cold front. This system helped to generate showers and storms across the region. One area of showers and storms produced up to two inches of rain on average in a short period of time in Princeton. Flash flooding ensued with four to six inches of water flowing over Stafford Drive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flowing water four to six inches deep across Stafford Drive. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms formed during the midday in Ohio, well in advance of a cold front in the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes. These storms raced east into central and northern West Virginia at 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  Their energy was derived mostly by those winds aloft. Brief severe wind gusts reached the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum wind gusts estimated around 100 mph touched down near Route 310 north of Grafton and just east of the Tygart River.  The storm continued through the Wickwire Valley, then northeast through mostly uninhabitable terrain.  The track ended in the Twin Oaks subdivision, just west of Route 119. ||There were no fatalities or injuries. The damage was mostly snapped and uprooted healthy trees.  One manufactured home was destroyed after a large tree fell through its roof.  A carport was also destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms formed during the midday in Ohio, well in advance of a cold front in the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes. These storms raced east into central and northern West Virginia at 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  Their energy was derived mostly by those winds aloft. Brief severe wind gusts reached the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused trees to fall onto power lines and houses.  The Washington Bottom area was hit hard. A teenage girl was hit by a fallen tree branch in the City Park for Parkersburg.  She had minor injuries.  It was observed that most of the old large trees that fell in the park had extensive rot within their trunks. ||Around a dozen homes had some degree of damage, mostly to their roofs. Power outages were widespread.  Some residents were without electricity for 4 days. At least one vehicle in the Washington Bottom vicinity was smashed by a fallen tree.  ||Embedded microbursts occurred in the Washington Bottom area.  The straight line wind damage continued with scattered wind damage into the city of Parkersburg.||One concentrated microburst was mostly confined to a wooded residential lot across from the Washington United Methodist Church on DuPont Road.  Many large tree trunks and large tree limbs were snapped.  It is in this vicinity that a maximum wind gust around 95 mph was estimated.  Another concentrated area of tree damage was around the intersection of Riverview Drive and DuPont Road.  Trees tops were snapped off.  There were 2 wooden phone poles that were snapped off close to the ground.  However, some wood rot was observed within those poles.  Winds of 70 to 75 mph were estimated around the City Park in Parkersburg.  Several of the large trees within the park had extensive wood rot.  Six residents stayed at a Red Cross shelter set up in the South Parkersburg Baptist Church.||Prior to crossing the Ohio River, this same storm caused tree damage in the Little Hocking vicinity of Ohio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On a hot and humid 14th, thunderstorms moved into the northern half of West Virginia during the afternoon, but did not reach southern counties until the overnight hours. Downpours were common.  Rain amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches occurred in many spots, but since the ground was relatively dry, no flooding occurred.||Lightning in the vicinity of a hilltop home in Putnam County between 2300 on the 13th to 0200E on the 14th is suspected of igniting a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large brick house on a hilltop off of Blue Lick Road caught fire and burnt to the ground.  Local and county officials suspect lightning ignited a fire in the attic.  A smoke detector went off alerting the 2 sleeping adults.  They got their 7 year old son and evacuated the house.||By the time fire crews arrived, the home was fully engulfed by flames.  No fire hydrants were located on the hilltop.  The home was a total lose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system crossed through Ohio into southern Pennsylvania during the daylight hours of the 12th.  Temperatures were in the 50s and 60s ahead of its associated cold front.  The cold front swept through West Virginia during the afternoon hours.  In the wake of the front, falling temperatures and strong pressure rises resulted in widespread wind gusts near 45 mph.  A few stronger gusts were measured .  A gust of 54 mph was measured on top of Bee Mountain in Kanawha County around 1417E.  A gust to 53 mph was measured at Yeager Airport near Charleston shortly after 1400E.  A gust to 52 mph was measured at the Buckhannon Airport in Upshur County around 1415E.  A gust to 51 mph was measured at Raleigh County Airport near Beckley just after 1400E.  A 51 mph gust was also measured at Elkins around 1530E. ||Power outages were most numerous in Wayne, Fayette, Wyoming, and Nicholas Counties.  Each county had over 2,000 customers without electricity for a short time.  In Kanawha County, a few homes were damaged by fallen trees or had siding or gutters pealed away.  One evergreen tree crashed through the attic of a house in the hills of Charleston and broke through the ceiling.  Strong cross winds tore roofing off a double wide mobile home being pulled along Interstate 77, leaving debris scattered across the roadway.  A 2 foot diameter tree fell and damaged fencing in the Teays Valley region of Putnam County.

A strong north to south temperature gradient existed in the Ohio Valley as a cold front gradually sank south.  Around dawn on the 2nd, the front sliced through West Virginia, near Clarksburg on southwest toward Huntington.  Strong dynamics associated with a strengthening wind speed maximum in the flow well above the ground, lead to waves along the front.  Each wave enhanced the precipitation and helped push the surface front further south.  ||Rain changed to freezing rain across north central and the Ohio Valley counties in West Virginia during the late morning into the mid afternoon.  The transition to freezing rain did not reach the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and the Kanawha River Valley around Charleston until 2000E on the 2nd.  After 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the southern coal fields and the southern mountains, the transition to freezing rain occurred around 0000E on the 3rd.||The duration of the freezing rain was mostly 1 to 3 hours before sleet and snow became the dominate precipitation type.  Most ice accretion amounts were under a quarter of an inch.  However, a quarter to a half inch of ice did accumulate in portions of the upper Guyandotte and upper Tug Fork River Valleys.  ||The last and main wave along the frontal zone enhanced the snow during the predawn hours on the 3rd.  This enhanced snow hit mostly the southern counties on into the central mountains of West Virginia during the morning.  The Route 50 corridor was on the northern fringe of this snow.  The snow tapered off from north to south during the late morning into the late afternoon on the 3rd, followed by rapid clearing.||The end result was a widespread snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches in less than 12 hours, with 4 to 8 inches in the central mountains and the upper Greenbrier River Valley.  However, a spotter in Persinger of Nicholas County did report 9 inches. Other maximum snow accumulations included 8 inches from the cooperative observers at Summersville and Mount Nebo.  Marlinton had a 6 inch accumulation.||The quick drop in temperature after the initial rain and freezing rain, made it difficult to remove the accumulating snow from roadways.  Readings dropped from the 30s into the teens during this storm. The governor shutdown the state government, telling all its workers in Charleston to stay at home.  County governments and school systems also were closed for the day.||On the evening of the 3rd, well after the snow had ended, several large boulders came loose from a cut away cliff along Brook Street in Williamson. The boulders rolled only a short distance and heavily damaged a parked car.  There was minor damage to the back of a house too.  This was in the area that received the heavier rain at the onset of the storm.        ||In the wake of the storm, a clear sky allowed temperatures to drop mostly into the single digits for the overnight hours into dawn on the 4th.  Yet, the cold spot was Elkins with observed record setting 10 degrees below zero.

A deep low pressure system affecting the Atlantic coast was followed by a strong arctic cold front passing across the region during the early morning hours of January 3rd, resulting in brief but heavy accumulating snow confined mainly to the west-facing slopes from southeast West Virginia through the mountains of North Carolina.  Winds with the arctic surge topped 60 mph in a few places across the mountains and, when combined with snow, resulted in periods of localized blizzard-like conditions.  Temperatures dropped into the teens and the positive single digits across the mountains, resulting flash-freezing of wet surfaces from rainfall on January 2nd.  Wind chills across the mountains were exceptionally cold, dropping into the negative teens.  Gradient winds began to relax by sunrise on the 3rd, allowing snow shower activity and blowing snow to diminish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down at Pinnacle Rock State Park.  The strong winds also resulted in wind chill temperatures ranging from -10F to -15F during the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon temperatures reached into the 70 to 75 degree range along the lower Elk River Valley.  Southwest winds were blowing at 10 to 15 mph. The relative humidity was around 25 percent. ||A 67 year old man near Clendenin was burning some debris near his home.  The wind caused the fire to flare up.  When firefighters arrived, a barn had caught fire and a small brush fire was burning over a hillside. The firefighters found the man collapsed on the ground.  His clothes were not burnt. The preliminary finding was the man died of cardiac arrest, apparently while trying to control the brush fire.

Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over southwest Virginia. During the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and northwest winds increased only to reinforce the cold air across the area. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be just east of the WV/VA border.||Snowfall totals averaged 12 to 17 inches across the region, with the highest amounts along the border with Virginia in Monroe County where some 18 inch amounts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 14 inches across the southern part of the county to 17 inches across the northern part. A tractor trailer overturned at mile marker 16.5 along I-77 and sustained indirect damage as a result of a snow covered road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong north to south temperature gradient existed in the Ohio Valley as a cold front gradually sank south.  Around dawn on the 2nd, the front sliced through West Virginia, near Clarksburg on southwest toward Huntington.  Strong dynamics associated with a strengthening wind speed maximum in the flow well above the ground, lead to waves along the front.  Each wave enhanced the precipitation and helped push the surface front further south.  ||Rain changed to freezing rain across north central and the Ohio Valley counties in West Virginia during the late morning into the mid afternoon.  The transition to freezing rain did not reach the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and the Kanawha River Valley around Charleston until 2000E on the 2nd.  After 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the southern coal fields and the southern mountains, the transition to freezing rain occurred around 0000E on the 3rd.||The duration of the freezing rain was mostly 1 to 3 hours before sleet and snow became the dominate precipitation type.  Most ice accretion amounts were under a quarter of an inch.  However, a quarter to a half inch of ice did accumulate in portions of the upper Guyandotte and upper Tug Fork River Valleys.  ||The last and main wave along the frontal zone enhanced the snow during the predawn hours on the 3rd.  This enhanced snow hit mostly the southern counties on into the central mountains of West Virginia during the morning.  The Route 50 corridor was on the northern fringe of this snow.  The snow tapered off from north to south during the late morning into the late afternoon on the 3rd, followed by rapid clearing.||The end result was a widespread snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches in less than 12 hours, with 4 to 8 inches in the central mountains and the upper Greenbrier River Valley.  However, a spotter in Persinger of Nicholas County did report 9 inches. Other maximum snow accumulations included 8 inches from the cooperative observers at Summersville and Mount Nebo.  Marlinton had a 6 inch accumulation.||The quick drop in temperature after the initial rain and freezing rain, made it difficult to remove the accumulating snow from roadways.  Readings dropped from the 30s into the teens during this storm. The governor shutdown the state government, telling all its workers in Charleston to stay at home.  County governments and school systems also were closed for the day.||On the evening of the 3rd, well after the snow had ended, several large boulders came loose from a cut away cliff along Brook Street in Williamson. The boulders rolled only a short distance and heavily damaged a parked car.  There was minor damage to the back of a house too.  This was in the area that received the heavier rain at the onset of the storm.        ||In the wake of the storm, a clear sky allowed temperatures to drop mostly into the single digits for the overnight hours into dawn on the 4th.  Yet, the cold spot was Elkins with observed record setting 10 degrees below zero.

A strengthening low pressure system crossed through Ohio into southern Pennsylvania during the daylight hours of the 12th.  Temperatures were in the 50s and 60s ahead of its associated cold front.  The cold front swept through West Virginia during the afternoon hours.  In the wake of the front, falling temperatures and strong pressure rises resulted in widespread wind gusts near 45 mph.  A few stronger gusts were measured .  A gust of 54 mph was measured on top of Bee Mountain in Kanawha County around 1417E.  A gust to 53 mph was measured at Yeager Airport near Charleston shortly after 1400E.  A gust to 52 mph was measured at the Buckhannon Airport in Upshur County around 1415E.  A gust to 51 mph was measured at Raleigh County Airport near Beckley just after 1400E.  A 51 mph gust was also measured at Elkins around 1530E. ||Power outages were most numerous in Wayne, Fayette, Wyoming, and Nicholas Counties.  Each county had over 2,000 customers without electricity for a short time.  In Kanawha County, a few homes were damaged by fallen trees or had siding or gutters pealed away.  One evergreen tree crashed through the attic of a house in the hills of Charleston and broke through the ceiling.  Strong cross winds tore roofing off a double wide mobile home being pulled along Interstate 77, leaving debris scattered across the roadway.  A 2 foot diameter tree fell and damaged fencing in the Teays Valley region of Putnam County.

Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over southwest Virginia. During the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and northwest winds increased only to reinforce the cold air across the area. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be just east of the WV/VA border.||Snowfall totals averaged 12 to 17 inches across the region, with the highest amounts along the border with Virginia in Monroe County where some 18 inch amounts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 12 inches across the western part of the county to 18 inches along the eastern part near the Virginia border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and fast moving storm system swept across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland from late evening of the 4th into late morning of the 5th. Snowfall from 4 to 10 inches fell along and north of a line from Zanesville in Ohio, to Wheeling in West Virginia, and from Pittsburgh to Latrobe in Pennsylvania. The snow then changed to sleet and freezing rain in the early morning hours of the 5th, with sleet accumulations of an inch or more, and freezing rain accretion from one quarter to one half inch. Outside of this region freezing rain was reported up to one half inch in parts of Greene county in Pennsylvania, and Monroe county in Ohio. Sleet accumulated more than 2 inches across the Fayette county ridges and in Garrett county Maryland, topped by about one quarter inch of freezing rain. Across northern West Virginia south of the Pennsylvania border, warmer air at the surface limited freezing rain amounts to around one tenth of an inch with a mix of sleet as well.

A strengthening low pressure system crossed through Ohio into southern Pennsylvania during the daylight hours of the 12th.  Temperatures were in the 50s and 60s ahead of its associated cold front.  The cold front swept through West Virginia during the afternoon hours.  In the wake of the front, falling temperatures and strong pressure rises resulted in widespread wind gusts near 45 mph.  A few stronger gusts were measured .  A gust of 54 mph was measured on top of Bee Mountain in Kanawha County around 1417E.  A gust to 53 mph was measured at Yeager Airport near Charleston shortly after 1400E.  A gust to 52 mph was measured at the Buckhannon Airport in Upshur County around 1415E.  A gust to 51 mph was measured at Raleigh County Airport near Beckley just after 1400E.  A 51 mph gust was also measured at Elkins around 1530E. ||Power outages were most numerous in Wayne, Fayette, Wyoming, and Nicholas Counties.  Each county had over 2,000 customers without electricity for a short time.  In Kanawha County, a few homes were damaged by fallen trees or had siding or gutters pealed away.  One evergreen tree crashed through the attic of a house in the hills of Charleston and broke through the ceiling.  Strong cross winds tore roofing off a double wide mobile home being pulled along Interstate 77, leaving debris scattered across the roadway.  A 2 foot diameter tree fell and damaged fencing in the Teays Valley region of Putnam County.

After several days of dry weather, a few brush fires were observed on the 21st into the 22nd. A brush fire in southern Roane County caused 1 firefighter from the Walton VFD to be overcome by heat exhaustion.  That brush fire was in the vicinity of Lewis Station and Payne Ridge Roads.||Another brush fire was along the steep north slope of the New River Gorge in Fayette County.  The fire was just upstream of the New River Bridge and Fern Creek.  It burned over 100 acres on the 21st into the 23nd.  Personnel from the Mt Hope Fire Department, National Park Service, and the Midewin Illinois Hotshots were used to control the fire.  A few hiking trails in the park were closed.

An intensifying low pressure system riding along the east coast brought snow to areas east of I-79 overnight on the 12th through the 13th. As the low strengthened and moved northward, a deformation zone brought heavy snow of rates over an inch an hour across the mountains of West Virginia and Maryland. The deformation band sat over Tucker county in WV and Garrett county MD the longest, bringing 15-20 inches of snow countywide. Preston county also experienced 6-10 inches of snow before the heaviest band moved east. The counties adjacent to Preston, Tucker, and Garrett received 5-7 inches of snow through the afternoon on the 13th.

Low pressure quickly developed over the lower Ohio Valley the night of the 2nd and moved northeast along a stalled Arctic front across West Virginia the morning of the 3rd. This brought a band of heavy snow across northern West Virginia, extreme southwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. Amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with highest totals of one foot of snow in Wetzel and Greene counties in Pennsylvania, and 10 inches in Garrett county Maryland, and Monongalia county in West Virginia. Just north of the heavy band of snow a general 2 to 5 inches of snow fell along a line east of Zanesville to downtown Pittsburgh, and south of the town of Indiana. There was a sharp edge to the snow with little accumulation north of this area.

Around dawn on the 2nd, the front sliced through West Virginia, near Clarksburg on southwest toward Huntington.  Strong dynamics associated with a strengthening wind speed maximum in the flow well above the ground, lead to waves along the front.  Each wave enhanced the precipitation and helped push the surface front further south.  ||Rain changed to freezing rain across north central and the Ohio Valley counties in West Virginia during the late morning into the mid afternoon.  The transition to freezing rain did not reach the Tygart River Valley around Elkins and the Kanawha River Valley around Charleston until 2000E on the 2nd.  After 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell across the southern coal fields and the southern mountains, the transition to freezing rain occurred around 0000E on the 3rd.||The duration of the freezing rain was mostly 1 to 3 hours before sleet and snow became the dominate precipitation type.  Most ice accretion amounts were under a quarter of an inch.  However, a quarter to a half inch of ice did accumulate in portions of the upper Guyandotte and upper Tug Fork River Valleys.  ||The last and main wave along the frontal zone enhanced the snow during the predawn hours on the 3rd.  This enhanced snow hit mostly the southern counties on into the central mountains of West Virginia during the morning.  The Route 50 corridor was on the northern fringe of this snow.  The snow tapered off from north to south during the late morning into the late afternoon on the 3rd, followed by rapid clearing.||The end result was a widespread snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches in less than 12 hours, with 4 to 8 inches in the central mountains and the upper Greenbrier River Valley.  However, a spotter in Persinger of Nicholas County did report 9 inches. Other maximum snow accumulations included 8 inches from the cooperative observers at Summersville and Mount Nebo.  Marlinton had a 6 inch accumulation.||The quick drop in temperature after the initial rain and freezing rain, made it difficult to remove the accumulating snow from roadways.  Readings dropped from the 30s into the teens during this storm. The governor shutdown the state government, telling all its workers in Charleston to stay at home.  County governments and school systems also were closed for the day.||On the evening of the 3rd, well after the snow had ended, several large boulders came loose from a cut away cliff along Brook Street in Williamson. The boulders rolled only a short distance and heavily damaged a parked car.  There was minor damage to the back of a house too.  This was in the area that received the heavier rain at the onset of the storm.        ||In the wake of the storm, a clear sky allowed temperatures to drop mostly into the single digits for the overnight hours into dawn on the 4th.  Yet, the cold spot was Elkins with observed record setting 10 degrees below zero.

Cold high pressure was in place across the region on Tuesday, February 11, 2014. An area of low pressure progressed northward from the Gulf of Mexico the 11th into the 12th of February and transported a generous amount of moisture into and across the cold air over the area allowing the precipitation to fall as moderate to heavy snow. During Tuesday night into Wednesday the low passed over southwest Virginia. During the day on Thursday, February 13, 2014, the low pressure progressed northeast of the region, and northwest winds increased only to reinforce the cold air across the area. Before its conclusion, there was a time where a clearly defined band of snow stalled, and pivoted around a central location in southwest Virginia. This allowed for the axis of heaviest snowfall to be just east of the WV/VA border.||Snowfall totals averaged 12 to 17 inches across the region, with the highest amounts along the border with Virginia in Monroe County where some 18 inch amounts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 12 inches over the eastern part of the county to 17 inches across the western and southern sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure quickly developed over the lower Ohio Valley the night of the 2nd and moved northeast along a stalled Arctic front across West Virginia the morning of the 3rd. This brought a band of heavy snow across northern West Virginia, extreme southwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. Amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with highest totals of one foot of snow in Wetzel and Greene counties in Pennsylvania, and 10 inches in Garrett county Maryland, and Monongalia county in West Virginia. Just north of the heavy band of snow a general 2 to 5 inches of snow fell along a line east of Zanesville to downtown Pittsburgh, and south of the town of Indiana. There was a sharp edge to the snow with little accumulation north of this area.

Low pressure moving toward the Mid Atlantic States produced an area of snow across northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland, with heavy snow falling in Tucker county where 6 to 10 inches accumulated. 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across portions of Monongalia, Marion, and Preston counties in West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland. There was a sharp gradient to the snow with little accumulation north of this region.

An upper level disturbance combined with a warm and humid air mass produced scattered severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-Atlantic from west to east. Hot and humid conditions were ahead of the frontal boundary and led to an unstable environment. Severe thunderstorms were capable of damaging winds.
Hot and humid conditions led to an unstable environment across the Mid Atlantic. Showers and Thunderstorms were capable of producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several large trees snapped ten to fifteen feet off the ground and multiple power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the western Atlantic led to southerly flow across the region. This led to hot and humid conditions ahead of an advancing cold front. Showers and thunderstorms moved across the region and some thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down on Minden Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the western Atlantic led to southerly flow across the region. This led to hot and humid conditions ahead of an advancing cold front. Showers and thunderstorms moved across the region and some thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down on Potter Road at Old Jersey Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the Mid-Atlantic from Delaware and showers and thunderstorms formed on the warm side where temperatures were near 90 and humid conditions led to heavy rain in activity.

An upper level disturbance moved along a stalled boundary across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and into western Pennsylvania producing numerous severe thunderstorms. An EF0 tornado also occurred over Clarion county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-Atlantic from west to east. Hot and humid conditions were ahead of the frontal boundary and led to an unstable environment. Severe thunderstorms were capable of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a tree down on Leisure Acres Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms formed during the midday in Ohio, well in advance of a cold front in the Midwest and Lower Great Lakes. These storms raced east into central and northern West Virginia at 40 to 45 mph during the afternoon.  Their energy was derived mostly by those winds aloft. Brief severe wind gusts reached the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches fell onto power lines and across Route 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a slow moving mid level trough in the Missouri, warm and humid air returned to northeast Kentucky on the 10th.  Sunshine helped temperatures reach into the mid 80s during the afternoon.  An arch of thunderstorms moved northeast out of Tennessee during the late afternoon, through eastern Kentucky and into West Virginia near sunset.  A well defined shelf cloud was observed on the leading edge of the squall line.  Wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph were widespread.  For example, the Tri State Airport near Huntington had a gust to 51 mph at 1909E, a spotter near Leon had a 45 mph gust around 1955E, and Charleston had a 46 mph gust near 2030E.  A few spots received slightly stronger wind gusts.  Electrical power outages occurred.  Cabell County and the Huntington vicinity had about 16,000 customers without electricity.       ||After an overnight and morning lull, more rounds of convection developed during midday on the 11th in southwest Virginia and southern West Virginia.  Those showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through portions of West Virginia during the afternoon.||Additional clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved northeast through West Virginia during the overnight hours of the 11th into the 12th.  Some of these showers were elongated along their movement.  Local rains of 1.5 to 2 inches over wet terrain managed to caused minor flash flooding.||The final round of showers and thunderstorms came during the afternoon and early evening of the 13th, as the surface cold front was moving through.  Some minor urban and poor drainage flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered locations about the city had trees blown down.  Officials reported a total of 42 roads were blocked temporarily.  Two commercial signs were blown down.  One restaurant had a glass window broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance ahead of a cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland on the afternoon of July 8th. Two brief EF-1 tornadoes also occurred, one in Mercer county Pennsylvania, and another near the borders of Marion and Monongalia counties in West Virginia. A strong micro-burst also occurred in Mercer county Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance ahead of a cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland on the afternoon of July 8th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found an EF-1 tornado touched down just inside Marion County, with estimated winds from 90 to 100 MPH, and a sporadic path just over 2 miles that continued into Monongalia County. The maximum path width of 50 yards produced a narrow corridor of tornado damage, mainly to snapped or uprooted hardwood trees. One structure sustained damage when a tree fell onto a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepening over the Great Lakes produced areas of thunderstorms the afternoon and evening of July 27th, with some of the storms being severe across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and eastern Ohio. Strong down bursts with winds of 80 MPH occurred near New Martinsville, West Virginia, with another downburst with winds estimated over 100 MPH occurred in Tucker county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey found a micro-burst occurred in New Martinsville. Maximum winds were estimated to be around 80 MPH. Many trees were uprooted and snapped, as well as damage to roofs and the exteriors of homes. A porch collapsed off one home, and  mobile homes were damaged by falling trees, with one mobile home shifted off its foundation.

Low pressure deepening over the Great Lakes produced areas of thunderstorms the afternoon and evening of July 27th, with some of the storms being severe across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and eastern Ohio. Strong down bursts with winds of 80 MPH occurred near New Martinsville, West Virginia, with another downburst with winds estimated over 100 MPH occurred in Tucker county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey found a Macro-burst occurred in Tucker county near Pleasant Run. Winds were estimated to be from 105 to 110 MPH, with a path length of 0.75 miles, and a width of 225 yards. Substantial damage to hardwood trees and power lines was observed, with numerous trees snapped and uprooted, and large power lines taken down.
